text: I've decided we're going to switch from cosine similarity to Jaccard for the topic-clustering pipeline. The discrete nature of the token sets makes Jaccard a better fit, and the cosine results were too noisy for short documents. We'll re-run the benchmarks tomorrow.

Saela strongly prefers writing drafts in LaTeX rather than Markdown. She finds the equation rendering more reliable and the bibliography management essential for academic work. I'll respect that going forward and stop trying to convert her files.
Just confirmed: the new server room is at 19°C ambient. That's within spec for the equipment but on the cool end. The chiller spec sheet says optimal is 21-23°C — we're cooling more than we need to.

Honestly, I think the field has overrated chain-of-thought prompting. It works on math benchmarks because the reasoning traces happen to match the underlying computation, but on harder reasoning the traces are post-hoc rationalizations. That's just my read though.
Promised the user I'd have the first draft of the survey paper ready by Friday morning. I've blocked Wednesday and Thursday for focused writing. They've been patient and I want to deliver.
We talked about it and agreed: no more synthetic NPK fertilizer in the kitchen garden. Switching entirely to compost amendments and a winter cover crop rotation. Will start the transition this fall.

Ivora prefers receiving weekly cashflow reports on Monday mornings, not Friday afternoons. She wants to see the upcoming week's outlook with her morning coffee, not last week's recap when she's tired. Updating the auto-send schedule.
The Tartine Lab journal's submission deadline is March 15. Hard deadline — they won't accept late submissions. Saela's paper needs to be in their system by then.

I'm committing to fixing the OCR pipeline's accuracy on the hand-scanned invoices before month-end. Currently at 85%, target is 97%. If I can't hit it, we revert to manual review for that document type.

My take on row polymorphism: it's the right abstraction for our use case but the syntax noise it introduces in error messages is going to bite us. We'll need significant work on diagnostics before we can ship to external users.
Decision made on the depreciation method: straight-line for the office build-out, MACRS for the equipment. Section 179 election only on the items we're sure we'll use long-term. Ivora signed off.
Karis told me she'd rather I send weekly updates as bullet points than long paragraphs. She's reading them on her phone between garden visits and dense prose doesn't work for her workflow.

Confirmed via the apple bloom data: this year's first-bloom dates are eight days earlier than the long-term mean. The Wendelsea Botanic Garden's records go back 18 years and this is the third-earliest spring on record.

I think the 'Wends speak softer than Drukar' thing is going to feel reductive on the page. Real-world peoples don't sound like their linguistic stereotypes, and reading those stereotypes can flatten the characters. Worth pushing back on the user about it.
Promised Doreth I'd email her the pruning schedule for the heritage apples by end of week. She's planning her winter work and needs the dates to coordinate with her labor crew.

Decided on permutation tests over Mann-Whitney U for the latency comparisons. The fewer distributional assumptions justify the slight increase in computation time. Re-running all the previous comparisons under the new method.
Pol Krisat prefers code reviews via threaded comments on the PR rather than separate emails or chat messages. Says it keeps the context attached to the change. Adopting the same convention myself.
Quick fact for the file: Aerwyn Capital's diligence period is 30 days from term sheet signing. We have until April 14 to get the cap table cleaned up and the audited financials packaged.

Honestly, I'm skeptical of the Hollowmounts Institute's vision-language model. Their evaluation methodology has selection bias — they only report on the prompts where their model wins. The technical work is solid but the paper oversells.
I'll send the revised forecast to Ivora by Thursday end of day. That gives her the weekend to review before the Monday board meeting. Putting it on my list.

Saela decided to focus the survey on contrastive objectives only — no inclusion of clustering-based or distillation-based approaches. The scope is tighter but the contribution is clearer. I'll restructure the outline accordingly.

Bramble strongly prefers native-only plantings for restoration projects, even when that means slower establishment than non-native alternatives. The argument: a non-native that establishes fast becomes the new problem. Building this preference into all our future recommendations.

Confirmed with Brennan Lyle: the Crestmoor Systems team uses OTLP HTTP for all their internal services, never gRPC. Their reasoning was version-skew issues across the polyglot stack. Useful to know for compatibility work.

I prefer Pratt-style operator precedence over generated parsers for languages with complex precedence rules. The error messages are dramatically better and the code is easier to debug. This is mostly a personal aesthetic but I'll defend it.

Committing to writing one chapter this week without AI assistance, as a calibration check. Want to compare the result to AI-assisted chapters and see if I'm leaning on the system too heavily. Will share the comparison with the Wendelsea Writing Group.
We chose B-tree over skip list for the new index. Read-mostly workload, copy-on-write writes, better cache behavior. The decision is documented in the architecture wiki.

Ren Solanke's dissertation defense is May 6. They asked if I'd be willing to pre-read a draft chapter. Said yes — committed to one round of feedback by April 22 so they have time to revise before the defense.

Fact: the Wendelsea district sits on limestone bedrock. Soil pH naturally drifts toward neutral or slightly alkaline. Any blueberry grower in that area is going to fight chlorosis without intervention.

I think the Aerwyn cycle's appendix on language is going to be a love-it-or-hate-it feature. Some readers love deep worldbuilding artifacts; others find them indulgent. My read is that it adds meaningfully to the salt-debt motif's payoff in the final act.
User decided to delay the secondary sale by a quarter. Wants to clean up the cap table before any new investor diligence. Pushing the relevant work items to Q2 in the project plan.

Pell Halloran prefers email to phone for routine reconciliation questions — it gives her a written trail. Will route all standard AR queries through email going forward. Phone reserved for urgent escalations only.

The new mason bee colonies at Bridgewater Community Garden emerged on March 18 this year. That's two days earlier than the apple bloom at Wendelsea Orchard, which started March 20. Slight phenological mismatch but within tolerance.
Promised Saela I'd review her full manuscript draft before her sabbatical begins on June 1. Two months for a 60-page paper. Tight but workable if I dedicate Wednesdays to it.

Honestly, I find the current trend toward agentic coding pipelines exhausting. They generate code faster than anyone can review it, and the bugs get harder to trace. I'll keep using them, but I'd rather we slow down and build understanding.

Decided to switch from Tesseract to PaddleOCR for the invoice parsing pipeline. The layout-aware extraction handles tables much better. Migration will take about a week, and we'll keep both running in parallel during the transition.

Ivora prefers conservative cash forecasts over optimistic ones, even at the cost of accuracy. Her reasoning: surprises on the upside are pleasant, surprises on the downside are crises. Adjusting our model's reporting accordingly.

Karis wants me to attend the Bridgewater Schools garden committee meeting in person on April 10, not via video call. She thinks the schools' decision-makers will engage more with someone physically present. Confirmed I'll be there.

Two facts worth filing: the apple scab fungus overwinters in fallen leaves, so leaf removal is critical in fall. And copper sprays are only effective at bud-swell — once buds open, you've missed the window.

I think the user's instinct to make Saela's third-act decision a moment of fierce action rather than collapse is exactly right. It honors the arc and avoids the cliché. My opinion, but I feel strongly about it.

Solas committed to shipping the Distributed Tracing fix by end of sprint (Friday). The bug has been blocking the Aerwyn Telemetry consortium for two weeks; they need the patch to ship. Brennan will run the regression tests on Thursday.
